1: 'Every charge must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses' (Deuteronomy 17:6; 2 Corinthians 19:15): Paul applies the Mosaic legal standard to the third visit, framing it as a formal apostolic proceeding. The legal framework is Christologically significant: Jesus himself appealed to the two-witness principle in John 8:17-18, offering his own testimony and the Father's as the required two witnesses. Paul stands in the stream of this Christological application of Mosaic legal categories.
2: The warning to those who sinned earlier and to all others: Paul's authority is exercised as Christ's ambassador (2 Corinthians 5:20), and his coming visit carries the weight of Christ's own authority over the community. The 'I will not spare' (ou pheisomai) echoes God's own warnings throughout the OT (cf. Ezekiel 5:11). The apostle's discipline is not personal vengeance but the exercise of Christ's own lordship over his community through the apostolic office.
3: 'Since you are demanding proof that Christ speaks through me': the ultimate apostolic question. Paul's entire letter has been the evidence — but the Christological criterion for apostolicity is finally stated here: Christ speaks through Paul. The Christ who speaks is 'not weak in dealing with you but is powerful among you' (ou asthenei eis hymas alla dynatei en hymin). This is the risen, powerful Christ — present and active in the community that came into existence through Paul's preaching.
4: A direct revelation of the Christ-pattern: 'he was crucified in weakness (ex astheneias), yet he lives by the power of God (ek dynameoas theou).' The past crucifixion in weakness and the present life in God's power define the entire Christological structure of the letter: 2 Corinthians 4:10 (carrying the death of Jesus so his life may be manifested), 2 Corinthians 5:14-15 (one died for all so that the living might live for him), 2 Corinthians 12:9 (power made perfect in weakness). Paul and his co-workers are 'weak in him' (asthenoumen en auto) but 'will live with him' (zesomen syn auto) by God's power in dealing with the Corinthians — the resurrection-pattern operative in the apostolic community.
5: 'Examine yourselves to see whether you are in the faith. Test yourselves. Do you not realize that Jesus Christ is in you?' (Iesous Christos en hymin estin): the community's inner test is the presence of Christ within them. The indwelling Christ (cf. Galatians 2:20: 'it is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me'; Colossians 1:27: 'Christ in you, the hope of glory') is both the criterion and the evidence of genuine faith. The self-examination invited here is not introspection for its own sake but orientation toward the Christ who indwells.
6: 'I hope you will discover that we have not failed the test' (ouk esmen adokimoi): Paul's apostolic credentialing is bound to the community's spiritual life. If Christ is genuinely in them, he got there through Paul's gospel. The self-examination of the community is simultaneously the vindication of Paul's ministry — not because Paul needs vindication for personal reasons, but because the community's Christ-life is the evidence of the gospel's genuine operation through Paul.
7: Paul's prayer: that the community would do what is right, even if it makes Paul appear to have failed the test. The Christological precedent: Christ himself 'appeared' to have failed — condemned, crucified, abandoned — yet this was the moment of God's ultimate vindication. Paul's willingness to appear powerless (if no discipline is needed) reflects the same Christ-pattern: the apparent weakness of unenforced authority is acceptable when the community's good is served.
8: 'We cannot work against the truth, only for it' (ou gar dynametha ti kata tes aletheias alla hyper tes aletheias): the truth is Christ himself (John 14:6). Apostolic authority can only work in alignment with Christ's own direction — the apostle cannot exercise power contrary to the one in whose name the authority is given. Truth is not an abstract principle but the person of Christ, whose character and mission define the limits and direction of all genuine apostolic activity.
9: 'We are glad whenever we are weak and you are strong': the apostolic joy in the community's strength mirrors Christ's own purpose — not to establish apostolic dominance but to build up the community. The prayer for 'your complete restoration' (hymon katartisin) is the prayer of Christ for his people: the interceding Christ (Romans 8:34; Hebrews 7:25) seeks the community's wholeness and completion. Paul's prayer participates in Christ's own intercessory work.
10: Authority given by the Lord for building up, not tearing down (oikodomein eis oikodomen kai ouk eis kathairesen): the telos of all Christological authority is construction of the community. Christ himself came to build his church (Matthew 16:18), not to condemn the world but to save it (John 3:17). The apostle's authority is a delegated share in Christ's own constructive purpose; its exercise against the community would contradict the commission from which it is derived.
11: The closing exhortations — restoration, encouragement, one mind, peace — describe the community's life in Christ. 'The God of love and peace will be with you': the God characterized by love (agape) and peace (eirene) is the God revealed in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:19: God was in Christ reconciling; Romans 5:1: we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ). The promise of God's presence with the community is the Immanuel-promise fulfilled in Christ (Matthew 1:23; 2 Corinthians 28:20: I am with you always).
12: The holy kiss as the community's embodied greeting: the physical gesture of fictive kinship enacted among those who are united in Christ. The community constituted by Christ's body-and-blood (1 Corinthians 10:16-17; 2 Corinthians 11:23-26) expresses that constitutive union in mutual physical greeting. The holy kiss is a sacramental gesture — a bodily sign of the spiritual reality of belonging to one another in Christ.
13: All the saints with Paul send greetings: the wider body of Christ participating in the letter's closing. The apostolic community is not a solitary enterprise but the body of Christ spread across multiple communities, all united in the one Lord. The co-greeting witnesses to the catholicity of the church: the Christ who is one (1 Corinthians 8:6; Ephesians 4:5) creates one body that greets itself across geographical distance.
14: A direct revelation structured as a Trinitarian benediction: 'the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.' The three-person structure names the full triune God as the source of the community's life: Christ's grace (the gift of his saving work), God's love (the Father's eternal love expressed in giving the Son, John 3:16; Romans 5:8), and the Spirit's fellowship (koinonia — the participation in the divine life that the Spirit mediates, Philippians 2:1). The community's existence is grounded in and sustained by the full triune God whose self-giving is the structure of all reality.
